metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and resolve all `except urllib.error.HTTPError` sites outside acli_rest.py (at minimum: outbound_differ.py, apply_handlers.py, acli_graph.py, fetcher.py, inbound_probe.py, acli_cli_ops.py, dispatch_one.py, applier.py) — specify in the plan whether each should be migrated to `except JiraAPIError`, left as-is with a seam note, or converted to a sentinel-catching adapter.
findings.finding: Translating HTTPError→JiraAPIError at acli_rest.py will silently break at least 8 callers OUTSIDE acli_rest.py that catch raw urllib.error.HTTPError from REST-path calls (outbound_differ.py:134, apply_handlers.py:147, acli_graph.py:259/360/454, fetcher.py:445/491/528, inbound_probe.py:107, acli_cli_ops.py:136, dispatch_one.py:484, applier.py:752). Several implement critical logic — e.g., apply_handlers.py:151 explicitly comments that JiraAPIError catch misses raw HTTPError 404 and must stay raw. None of these are enumerated as edit targets or acknowledged as blast-radius consumers in the plan.
findings.location: What section (first bullet) / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow the translation to only the specific REST methods called by the retry-wired path (e.g., those invoked via _call_with_retry), or add an explicit enumeration of affected callers to the Scope section with a per-caller disposition (migrate / leave-raw / adapter). A blanket boundary translation without this audit will silently regress proven fault-tolerance code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan HOW the dedup-key check is incorporated per retry attempt for non-idempotent operations — e.g., wrap create_issue in a dedup-aware callable that re-runs the rebar-id JQL search before each retry, or restructure _call_with_retry to accept an idempotency-check hook, so AC2 ('timed-out create is not re-sent without a prior rebar-id search') is mechanically achievable.
findings.finding: The plan's write-safety invariant ('non-idempotent writes are never blindly retried before their dedup key exists') cannot be satisfied by translating HTTPError→JiraAPIError alone. _call_with_retry is a generic wrapper that simply re-invokes the same function; the dedup JQL search (dispatch_one.py:196) executes once before _call_with_retry(client.create_issue, …) is called, not before each retry attempt inside the wrapper. If create_issue receives a 429 or 5xx mid-flight (after the issue may or may not have been created), the retry re-invokes create_issue without re-running the JQL check, violating the invariant. The plan names the goal but does not specify the mechanism for achieving it within _call_with_retry's call structure.
findings.location: What section, first bullet ('ensure non-idempotent writes (create/comment) are only retried when a dedup key (rebar-id label / comment marker) already exists'); AC2 ('timed-out create is not re-sent without a prior rebar-id search')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare `_call_with_retry(client.create_issue, _ticket_data)` call with a dedup-aware wrapper: before each attempt, search for `labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"` and return the existing key if found (confirming the binding), otherwise proceed with create_issue. This makes the dedup check per-attempt rather than per-call-site.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `JiraAPIError` with an optional `retry_after: float | None` field (populated from `HTTPError.headers.get('Retry-After')` at the `acli_rest.py` translation seam) and update `_call_with_retry` to sleep `exc.retry_after` when set, so AC1's 'Retry-After honored' assertion is achievable.
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 asserts '_call_with_retry demonstrably retries a simulated 429 with Retry-After honored' for the REST path, but JiraAPIError (the translation target at acli_rest.py) only carries status_code — it has no retry_after field — so the Retry-After header value from urllib.error.HTTPError is silently dropped at the translation seam and _call_with_retry has no mechanism to read it.
findings.location: AC1 / acli_rest.py translation seam / _errors.py:42-44 / dispatch_one.py:100-144
findings.suggested_fix: In `_errors.py`, add `retry_after: float | None = None` to `JiraAPIError.__init__`. In `acli_rest.py`, at the HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation point, extract `float(http_err.headers.get('Retry-After', 0)) or None` and pass it. In `_call_with_retry`, replace the fixed `delays[attempt]` sleep with `exc.retry_after if exc.retry_after else delays[attempt]` when `exc.status_code == 429`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via a probe or Jira REST API docs citation) that Jira Cloud REST 429 responses include a `Retry-After` header, and specify in the plan how `urllib.error.HTTPError.headers` is accessed to extract it — or note (as ADR 0036 did) that the implementation is defensive (honors it only when present, falls back to jittered backoff otherwise).
findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'Retry-After honored' for the REST-path 429 without verifying that (a) Jira's REST API actually includes a Retry-After header on 429 responses and (b) urllib.error.HTTPError reliably exposes it. ADR 0036 made the analogous caveat explicit for the subprocess path ('the exact ACLI 429 exit code and whether Retry-After is emitted, are provider-dependent and could not be verified on demand'). The _call_with_retry helper in dispatch_one.py currently uses only fixed delays and has no Retry-After extraction logic — so this is a new capability asserted without a prior-art citation or probe result.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1: 'a simulated 429 with Retry-After honored'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note analogous to ADR 0036 §'Probe disposition': 'Retry-After is honored iff present in the urllib.error.HTTPError response headers (HTTPError.headers["Retry-After"]); if absent, jittered exponential backoff is used. If Jira does not emit Retry-After, the fallback fires silently — no behavioral regression.' This resolves the unverified-capability assertion without requiring a live probe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `bind_pending` records include the newly-created issue key (returned by `create_issue`) so recovery can locate the unlabeled orphan directly by key rather than by rebar-id label search, and add an AC that recovery attaches the label and confirms the record when an issue key is present in the pending entry.
findings.finding: The recovery path relies on `search_issues` keyed on the `rebar-id` label, but an issue that crashed between `create_issue` and label-attach has NO `rebar-id` label yet — a label-based search cannot find it, so the orphan is invisible to recovery and the next pass will create a duplicate, defeating the entire AC3 guarantee.
findings.location: AC3 / Why §1 / binding_store.py / dispatch_one.py:241-256
findings.suggested_fix: After `create_issue` returns `issue_key`, write `bind_pending(rebar_id, issue_key, status='pending')` immediately. During recovery, for any pending record that already has an `issue_key`, call `add_label(issue_key, rebar_id)` directly — no search needed — then confirm. Only for records without an `issue_key` (crash before create returned) is a search warranted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden AC6 (or add an AC6b) to include the unit test directories that cover the new mechanisms: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/ -q`, or alternatively specify that the new tests for steal/bind_pending/retry-translation must reside in tests/scripts/bridge/.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q` as the green-bar gate, but the new mechanism tests for steal(), bind_pending write-ahead, and HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation will naturally extend existing test suites in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/ and tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/ — directories NOT covered by the AC6 command. If the implementation author adds tests only in those existing suites (the path of least resistance), AC6 would pass vacuously without covering any of the new logic.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6 / Scope (tests)
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC6 to: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/ -q` to cover all directories where the new mechanism tests naturally land.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC (or sub-criterion in AC1) that exercises the HTTPError→JiraAPIError→_call_with_retry chain against a real HTTP-level stub (e.g., `responses` library or a local httpretty fixture that returns a genuine 429 status) rather than a manually constructed exception, so the proving command cannot pass while the actual REST call path is untranslated.
findings.finding: The single proving command `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q` is the only verification mechanism in the plan; it is a mock/offline proxy that cannot demonstrate the HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation fires on the actual requests/acli_rest.py code path as it runs against a real (or integration-test-level) HTTP response — satisfying the criterion without the cutover path ever executing end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC6; Scope — 'acli_rest.py' cutover
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'verified by a test that uses the `responses` (or `httpretty`) library to return HTTP 429 / 500 at the network layer and asserts that _call_with_retry fires the expected retry count — not by directly raising HTTPError in test code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1 to specify the retry-exhaustion contract: what exception type is raised to the caller after all retries are consumed, and add a test that asserts this (e.g., 'after N retries a JiraAPIError with the final status code is raised').
findings.finding: AC1 specifies that _call_with_retry 'demonstrably retries a simulated 429 with Retry-After honored, and a simulated 500 with jittered backoff' but does not specify the observable contract when all retries are exhausted — what exception type propagates to the caller, whether it is a JiraAPIError or something else, and whether it is distinguishable from a non-retried failure. This leaves a new user-facing failure path (retry exhaustion) with no verifiable acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'After all retries are consumed, a JiraAPIError (not a raw HTTPError) is raised to the caller, with the HTTP status code preserved; this is covered by a test that simulates N consecutive 500s.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'alert' and 'manifest tally' in AC4 concretely: e.g., state the alert is a log.error() call at ERROR level AND that 'manifest tally' means the integer count of recovery failures returned/accumulated in the reconcile pass summary object (or whichever mechanism already exists).
findings.finding: AC4 uses the term 'nonzero manifest tally' and 'raises an alert' without defining what a manifest tally is or what mechanism constitutes an alert (log line, metric increment, exception, etc.); an executor cannot implement or measure either concept.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'recover_pending_bindings receives a client object exposing search_issues; a recovery failure raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally instead of a silent swallow'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally' with something like: 'logs an ERROR-level message and increments the pass-level error_count returned by the reconcile pass (so callers observe a nonzero error total and can exit nonzero)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-handling requirement for `bind_pending` write failure: specify that a failure to write the pending record MUST abort the create_issue call (raise loudly, fail the task item, do not proceed), and add a test covering this path.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the error path when `bind_pending` itself fails (e.g., disk full, permission error on the store file) — it is silent on whether `create_issue` should be aborted, the pass should fail loudly, or the error should be swallowed.
findings.location: AC3 / dispatch_one.py / binding_store.py callers
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the bind_pending + persist sequence in a try/except; on failure, log at ERROR level, increment a manifest error counter, and raise to the caller so create_issue is never reached — consistent with the 'loud failure' principle already applied to recover_pending_bindings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that steal() blocks synchronously for up to lease_secs seconds and specify the intended handling — e.g., whether a diagnostic log/print is emitted during the wait, whether the caller uses a non-default sleep_fn, or whether a two-phase design (detect expiry → exit with retriable code → re-run acquires) is preferred to avoid blocking the scheduler.
findings.finding: The plan calls steal() in __main__.py's held-lock path but does not acknowledge that steal() blocks synchronously for up to lease_secs (default 120 s) before attempting the CAS. Invoking this synchronously in the scheduler's lock-check path turns what was an instant exit-3 into a 2-minute hang on every expired-lock re-run; if a CI scheduler's job timeout is shorter than the lease, the steal is never attempted. The plan gives no guidance on whether steal() is called inline or via a retry-invocation pattern.
findings.location: What section, third bullet ('In __main__.py's held-lock path, invoke steal() when the lease is expired'); AC5 ('A pass encountering an expired lease steals it via steal() and proceeds')

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback schema-state assertion: specify what bindings.json looks like after a rollback (e.g., that pending entries with null jira_key are safe for pre-plan code, or provide a one-shot migration/cleanup that converts pending→absent on rollback), and document that `is_bound()` returning True for a null-jira_key entry is handled by the caller.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the schema state of bindings.json after a rollback: if code is rolled back after a `bind_pending` + `save()` call has written a `{state: 'pending', jira_key: null}` entry (e.g., crash between pending-save and create_issue), the pre-plan code will see that entry as `is_bound()=True` (key present in bindings dict) but with a null `jira_key`, potentially suppressing a CREATE mutation forever — a silent data-loss path on partial failure + rollback.
findings.location: What / bind_pending section; AC: 'bind_pending is written and the store persisted before create_issue'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the `HTTPError`→`JiraAPIError` exception-contract change in the plan: add a brief audit note confirming no existing callers catch `HTTPError` by type (or list those that do and describe the migration), and describe a rollback path (e.g., keep HTTPError as a base/alias, or note that a revert commit is the back-out).
findings.finding: The plan translates `HTTPError` → `JiraAPIError` at `acli_rest.py` (a change to the exception contract at a shared boundary) without acknowledging whether existing callers in the codebase catch `requests.HTTPError` directly, and without describing an expand-contract sequence or rollback path if such callers exist.
findings.location: What section (first bullet), Scope ('In: acli_rest.py'), AC #1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a caller-impact acknowledgment: audit all call sites of acli_rest.py for existing HTTPError catches and document whether each must be updated (or confirm none exist) before the boundary translation is introduced.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a behavioral change at the acli_rest.py boundary — HTTPError is replaced with JiraAPIError — but does not acknowledge existing callers that may currently catch requests.HTTPError directly. If any caller in acli_subprocess.py, dispatch_one.py, or run_differs.py catches HTTPError, they will silently stop catching after this change, defeating the intent of the error-translation fix.
findings.location: Scope / What — 'Translate HTTPError → JiraAPIError at the client boundary (acli_rest.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Caller impact' note in the What section: 'Audit acli_rest.py callers (acli_subprocess.py, dispatch_one.py) for existing `except HTTPError` clauses; update or remove them as part of this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Why (identifies `_call_with_backoff` zero-call-sites as a problem) and What/Scope (neither describes wiring it nor explicitly defers it): either add a 'What' bullet describing how `_call_with_backoff` is wired into `_call_with_retry`, or add an explicit deferral sentence in Scope explaining why it is intentionally left for a later story.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section explicitly lists `_call_with_backoff` (zero production call sites) as one of three inert retry layers, but the 'What' section and all ACs address only two of the three layers (HTTPError translation + _call_with_retry revival). `_call_with_backoff` remaining a dead-code path is neither resolved nor explicitly deferred with justification.
findings.location: Why section (item 4) vs. What section (first bullet) vs. Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Scope: '`_call_with_backoff` wiring: `_call_with_retry` already delegates to it internally; no new call-site is needed beyond the HTTPError translation — confirm or explicitly defer if that assumption is wrong.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the three architectural contracts this plan wires: (1) HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation as the retry-enablement seam, (2) bind_pending-before-create as the write-ahead ordering invariant, and (3) steal()-on-expired vs exit-3-on-live as the lease fence semantics.
findings.finding: The plan establishes a new system-boundary contract (HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation at acli_rest.py), changes the write-ahead ordering protocol (bind_pending before create_issue), and changes lease-acquisition semantics (steal() on expiry) — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc note is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / AC lines 1–5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief `docs/adr/0NNN-reconciler-safety-wiring.md` (or equivalent CLAUDE.md block) that states: the client boundary at acli_rest.py is the single translation point for HTTP errors into JiraAPIError; bind_pending + store flush must precede any non-idempotent Jira write; steal() is the correct response to an expired lock and must not be replaced with an unconditional exit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE step to the binding store lifecycle: specify when confirmed pending records are deleted or compacted (e.g., purged after successful confirmation, or after a retention window), and ensure the plan's implementation includes a call to this cleanup path.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a RETIRE/cleanup path for confirmed binding-store pending records — entries transition pending→confirmed but are never removed, causing unbounded accumulation in the store over many reconciler passes.
findings.location: binding_store.py / AC3 / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that steal() sets a bounded TTL/expiry on the newly-written lease, and add a test scenario where the stealing process itself is killed to confirm the next pass can also steal the expired lease (i.e., the fix is self-healing across multiple crash generations).
findings.finding: The plan does not address what happens to the stolen lease when the stealing process itself fails (crash, SIGKILL, Jira down): the stolen lease is now held with no recovery path, re-introducing the same deadlock the plan is fixing.
findings.location: __main__.py lock path / AC5 / Why §3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify which acli_subprocess.py function the plan intends as the 'inert retry layer' (the name _call_with_backoff does not exist; the actual candidate is _rate_limit_backoff at line 122 or _run_acli's internal retry loop), and update the Why/What/Scope text to reference the real symbol and correct line range.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '_call_with_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:236-292) has zero production call sites' and lists acli_subprocess.py as an in-scope file to fix, but no function named _call_with_backoff exists anywhere in the codebase (search returns zero matches). The actual lines 236-292 in acli_subprocess.py contain _check_mutation_failure and _decode_partial. What does exist is _rate_limit_backoff (line 122), which IS called from _run_acli as part of C4 work. The plan's premise and fix scope for the 'inert retry layer' at acli_subprocess.py rests on a non-existent symbol.
findings.location: Why section, point 4 / Scope 'In:' list / acli_subprocess.py:236-292
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '_call_with_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:236-292)' with '_rate_limit_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:122-144) / _run_acli retry loop' and verify whether acli_subprocess.py actually needs changes or whether the HTTPError→JiraAPIError translation at acli_rest.py alone is sufficient to revive _call_with_retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the 'Why' section's line-number references for the recover_pending_bindings miswiring from 'run_differs.py:267' and '268-274' to the correct lines (currently 351 and 350-357 respectively) so implementors apply the fix at the right location.
findings.finding: The plan states 'run_differs.py:267 passes the applier module (which has no search_issues) as the client to recover_pending_bindings, so real recovery would AttributeError into a fail-open swallow (run_differs.py:268-274)'. The line numbers are wrong: the actual call is at line 351, the fail-open except block spans lines 350-357. While the substantive bug is confirmed, the misattributed line numbers constitute an unverified assertion about a specific code location that implementors will reference during surgery.
findings.location: Why section, point 2 / run_differs.py:267-274
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'run_differs.py:267 passes the applier module … (run_differs.py:268-274)' to 'run_differs.py:351 passes the applier module … fail-open swallow (run_differs.py:352-357)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
